metadata.summary: Framework for evaluating design systems via four pillars: ownership, usable components, documentation, and adoption. Enables quarterly health checks and OKRs.

content.title: Design System Success Factors
content.summary: Design systems fail most often not from technical debt but from governance and adoption gaps: no single owner, documentation that drifts from source, components teams work around. A four-pillar model makes failure modes explicit and measurable, enabling quarterly health checks and OKR-setting for platform teams. The pillars are: clear ownership (dedicated responsible person/team), usable components (adoptable API and quality), strong documentation (findable and current), and real adoption (measured component coverage across products).

why.p: Design systems fail most often not from technical debt but from governance and adoption gaps: no single owner, documentation that drifts from source, components teams work around. The four-pillar model makes failure modes explicit and measurable, enabling quarterly health checks and OKR-setting for platform teams.

list.item: Standing up a new design system or evaluating build vs. adopt vs. wrap.
Quarterly health check on an existing system: ownership, adoption, contribution, debt.
Diagnosing why a system is being ignored (low component coverage, parallel snowflake CSS).
Pre-merger or rebrand audit when two systems must be unified.
Setting OKRs/KPIs for a platform team.
One-off marketing landing pages — a tokens file + Tailwind config is sufficient.
Pre-product-market-fit prototyping — the pillars optimize for adoption, which assumes stable surface area.
Teams with fewer than 3 designers and 1 frontend engineer — ownership pillar collapses, overhead exceeds value.
Pure motion or brand-illustration systems — adoption metrics are not meaningful.

rule.statement: Assign a single named owner or team with authority and accountability for the design system before any other work begins.
rule.rationale: Systems with diffuse or absent ownership drift: no one decides breaking changes, documentation goes stale, and consumer teams lose trust and fork. Ownership is the first pillar because all others depend on it.

rule.statement: Components must be adoptable as-is by consumer teams without local overrides; if a component requires frequent customization to use, it is not usable enough.
rule.rationale: High override rates indicate the component does not fit real use cases. Components that need workarounds get bypassed — driving snowflake CSS growth rather than coverage improvement.

rule.statement: Documentation must be discoverable and current; a page count that drifts from source code is worse than no documentation because it actively misleads consumers.
rule.rationale: Stale docs erode trust faster than gaps. Consumers learn to distrust the docs and stop consulting them, creating a negative feedback loop where documentation becomes irrelevant.

rule.statement: Treat the system as a product with a roadmap and SLAs, not a side project; measure adoption monthly and publish coverage metrics visibly to consumer teams.
rule.rationale: Peer visibility of coverage metrics outperforms top-down mandates for driving adoption. Teams that see their coverage percentage relative to peers improve it without explicit directives.

section.name: mvp-launch-sequence
section.p: Start with the minimum viable system: core interactive components, design tokens (color, spacing, typography), brand colors, and a small pattern library. Launch, stress-test with one consumer product, gather feedback, then grow iteratively. Launching too large creates debt before validation.

section.name: adoption-metrics

rule.statement: Measure component coverage as the percentage of UI primitives (button, input, card, modal) sourced from the system versus implemented locally; count at the render site, not the import statement.
rule.rationale: Import-level counting is gameable — a team may import the Button and use it once while implementing 40 custom buttons locally. Render-site AST scanning via ts-morph provides the actual usage picture.

list.item: Component coverage: % of UI primitives using system components vs local implementations
Adoption rate: number of active consumer teams using the system package
Contribution rate: external PRs merged to the system per quarter
Bug/issue count: open issues by priority as a quality proxy
Design-dev sync: % of semantic tokens matching between Figma and code

section.reference: AST-based Node script counting system vs snowflake primitive usage across consumer apps

section.name: agent-automatable-audit-areas
section.p: Agents handle the measurable parts of the four pillars: documentation freshness (last-updated vs last-source-commit), token-design parity (Figma vs code), contribution telemetry (PR merge rates), and adoption scanning (AST import analysis across consumer repos). Ownership and "real adoption" framing require human review — agents cannot decide whether to fund a platform team.

rule.statement: Pair every component release with a codemod (jscodeshift) that migrates consumers; documentation-only releases erode trust and drive forks.
rule.rationale: Consumer teams that must manually migrate to a new API version lose confidence in the system's release discipline. Codemods remove the migration burden and increase actual adoption of new releases.

example.note: Coverage: "Crawl the apps/ workspace. For each consumer app, list components imported from @org/ui, count usages, and list visual primitives (button, input, card, modal) implemented locally instead. Emit coverage.json with { app, system_uses, snowflake_uses, coverage_pct }."

Docs freshness: "Given Storybook's index.json and the system's source src/components/*, list components with no Storybook story (docs gap) or whose story has not changed in 180+ days while source has."

antipattern.description: Agent fabricating adoption percentage by counting import statements without counting render sites.
antipattern.reason: A team may import the Button once and implement 40 custom buttons locally. Import-level counting inflates coverage metrics; render-site AST scanning (ts-morph) provides the actual figure.

antipattern.description: LLM generating components that re-implement existing system primitives because the catalogue was not provided as context.
antipattern.reason: Without the system's catalogue (Storybook index.json or package exports), the agent does not know what already exists and will write duplicate components that increase snowflake usage.

antipattern.description: Token rename PRs from agents missing CSS-in-JS template literals and Tailwind config references.
antipattern.reason: AST token rename tools typically handle import paths and property access, but miss string-interpolated CSS-in-JS patterns and Tailwind config keys. Require a second-pass regex sweep before merging.

antipattern.description: Agent-authored PRs merging to consumer app main branches without per-app release-train coordination.
antipattern.reason: Codemods to unrelated apps must respect their release schedules. Auto-merging into main of unrelated apps can ship breaking visual changes ahead of their release cycle.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Treat the system as a product with a roadmap, customers (consumer teams), and SLAs — not as a side-project of the platform team. Define adoption tiers (Bronze/Silver/Gold) per consumer app and publish them; peer pressure outperforms top-down mandates. Pair every component release with a codemod (jscodeshift) that migrates consumers; "documentation only" releases erode trust. Run a contribution day each quarter — measured contribution rate is meaningless without a working contribution path. Keep tokens and components in different release trains; tokens move slowly, components iterate. Make breaking changes loud (changesets `major`, codemod, deprecation warnings) and silent fixes silent (`patch`). Capture support-ticket categories per quarter; the dominant category is your next roadmap item.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.note: CLI tools: Style Dictionary (token build → CSS/JS/iOS/Android), Storybook + Chromatic (visual regression + docs), Backlight (token + component pipeline), zeroheight CLI (sync Figma libraries to docs), Knapsack (design-system platform CLI), jscodeshift / ts-morph (AST scanning for adoption metrics), Repo-stats / Sourcegraph (cross-repo usage queries), changesets (versioning + changelog discipline), Figma REST API (library publish / consumer counts).

Services & apps: Chromatic (SaaS, yes agent-friendly — CLI + GitHub Actions), zeroheight (SaaS, partial agent-friendly — REST API), Knapsack (SaaS, yes agent-friendly — CLI), Supernova.io (SaaS, yes agent-friendly — REST + CLI), Specify (SaaS, yes agent-friendly — REST + CLI), Tokens Studio (Figma plugin, limited), Backlight (SaaS, yes agent-friendly — CLI), Penpot (OSS, limited), Storybook OSS (yes agent-friendly), Figma Variables (SaaS, yes agent-friendly — REST API).

section.name: limitations
section.p: Adoption rate defined as "% of teams using the package" hides depth of usage; teams may import once and override everything. The four pillars are necessary but not sufficient — they say nothing about API design, theming model, or release cadence. Component coverage is gameable — counting "Button" once vs counting every variant inflates the metric. "Strong documentation" can be confused with "lots of documentation"; success depends on findability and currency, not page count. The MVP-first lean does not work in regulated industries where accessibility/legal review must precede first release.

template.purpose: AST-based Node script: counts system vs snowflake primitive usage across consumer apps.
